VerbalDescription: Recreate the same grid from the demonstration and the remove the randomly colored squares by completing the colored lines.
Keep the grid size the same. Remove all single colored squares from the grid and replace with the color of the line.

The rule is to take any out of place colored-squares (in this case orange squares) and color them the exact color of whichever row they are in. So in this case, on the second row down, you will color the 3rd orange square black. On the third row down, you will color the eighth orange square blue. On the seventh row down, you will color the third and eighth orange squares blue. On the eighth row down, you will color the seventh and eleventh orange square black. On the tenth row down, color the third orange square black. On the eleventh row down, color the ninth orange square blue.
Keep the grid the same size as the test input. "Clean up" the odd colored blocks so that they match with their corresponding row. You should have rows of alternating solid colors in the end.
look for the two colours that form the stripes. fill in the other colours to match the stripe leaving stripes of two colours

Copy the exact grid size and replicate the entire picture. Then delete any "random" squares that disrupt the columns and fill them in so it is a cohesive two colour grid with no random squares either out of line or differing in colour

Make your answer grid match the height and width of the test grid. You will see that there are horizontal or vertical appearing bands/lines of two different colours. You will also note that interspersed among the main lines are blocks of a different colour. There are far fewer blocks of this different colour, and they stand out on the grid you will be looking at. Remove they blocks and change colour so that the space now is coloured to match the main colour of the horizontal or vertical line of blacks. You should end up with what looks like interchanging vertical or horizontal lines. Each row or column, depending on orientation, will be a complete colour.
one shape expect other shape fill
Keep the grid the same size. Follow the striped pattern using the main 2 colors and get rid of the 3rd color that is interrupting the striped pattern.
Remove the oddly coloured blocks so that only the linear two-tone pattern remains unbroken, keeping it's colour shape, rotation and size.

Resize the grid to the same as the input. Keep everything exactly the same but make sure there are only two colours. For example: If a a row or column is mostly green then make sure all squares are green.

Your input should show a striped pattern with some dots on it. Your task is to remove these dots. I would recommend clicking copy from input and then covering these dots with the same colour as is in the line its on
Replace the odd coloured squares with another colour to complete the pattern
Remove the colours that are the least dominant.

reszie grid to 6x6, create a horizontal striped pattern in light blue and burgundy, starting with a light blue line across the whole grid, with a burgundy line directly underneath then repeat the pattern to fill the grid.
good correction
Copy from grid.  Follow the rule of the stripes.  If stripes are horizontal they should be the alternating colors.  If stripes are vertical they should be the alternating colors.

Keep the grid size the same. The object has colored lines either from top to bottom or left to right. After determining which pattern (top to bottom, left to right) the object has, complete the line pattern by changing the color on the grid to the dominating color in the line pattern. For example, if an orange line is interrupted with a blue square, change the blue square to orange to complete the line.

Copy the original grid exactly as it is. It should be two main colors, with a third color dispersed "randomly" throughout the design. Change that extra color to the stripe color underneath it, so it just looks like a two colored striped block.
Copy the input grid. Then edit the squares in the verticle or horizontal lines to become the same colour as the rest of the line.
Copy the grid size and image from the original, keep the same colour scheme. For any odd colour variation in the pattern, edit these to match the majority of the colours in that pattern.

Change the grid size to match the test input grid. In the test input grid, you should see alternating lines of two colours with random boxes of different colours, recreate this exactly in the output but change the differing colours to match what the rest of the column/row is doing
Copy the whole input grid. Keep the 2 lines that go all the way from end to end- one should go across and the other up and down. Keep only these 2 lines and make the other boxes that are red black.
Resize the grid to the target. Then search for a cross within the image. When you find that, just paint the cross into the image.

Use same grid size as input. Note the 1 continuous vertical and horizontal red line that can be seen in the pixels. There is only 1 vert and horiz red line that fills the entire grid height or width, respectively. Erase - i.e., color as black - all squares that aren't part of that complete vert red line and and horiz red line.
Resize the grid to match the sample.  Redraw the complete row and column, matching the color.

Change the size of your grid to match the input. Copy across the image to your new grid. Now remove any red squares that do not sit in a line that stretches from one side of the grid to another. You should be left with two straight lines at the end that cross over at a point.
In this task i copy the input and from there remove and coloured squares that do not connect in a straight line to the other side of the grid.

the solution first involves adjusting the width and height of the grid into the same as the input. We must then determine the "point" in the input that is connected by columns and rows of colour that are "unbroken" (continuous and not separated at any patches of black colour). Colour this point, along with its connecting, and continuous row and column with the same colour as in the input.

Keep grid size the same as input. In the input there will be a single row and column that intersects. Remove all other squares that are not part of these lines in the output. There will only be 2 lines left that intersect like a cross, or a T if at the boundary, in the output.

Keep the grid size and colors the same. Only copy the squares of the object that form a complete line that connects left to right, or top to bottom. If there is any interruption in the color of the line, then it doesn't get copied.

Use the same grid size. Look for the row that is a complete different color as well as the column that is a complete different color. You want to make all the other blocks black. You will end up with one orange column and one orange row, intersecting.
The input will be partially filled. Same grid size. In the same colour as the colourings of the input, fill in exactly the vertical and horizontal lines that connect from border-to-border.
Keep the grid the same size and the input. Find the longest horizontal and the longest vertical lines and input only them. Keep the colour the same as the input.
Copy the input from the demonstration grid. Find the blocks that form a complete line from top to bottom. Find the blocks that form a complete line from left to right. Delete all other blocks.
Remove all necessary blocks to ensure you are only left with two lines that intersect.
Use the same grid size as in the input. Only keep the lines that go from end to end and keep it's color the same
Keep the same grid. Keep the two longest connected grids intact and remove the remaining squares by coloring them black.
Copy the input and take out any squares that don't connect continuously from 1 edge to another.
keep same grid and colour from the input however the final shape should be the 2 longest line that reaches the edge of the grid and cross each other at 90 degrees
Set grid size to 12 by 12. Identify the two lines of red blocks. Transfer those to the 12 by 12 grid. Do not transfer the objects that are not in line.
Copy input.  Remove all red boxes that don't follow the two straight lines
NICE
I REMEMBERED THE EXAMPLE OF THE GIRD SIZE THE I CHANGED BY USING INPUT GIRD.
Change the grid size as on the input grid. Then click flood fill icon, and the color blue. Click on the grid.

Based on the task demonstration, all the minor different colours should be removed. Apply the same to the test input grid, removed all the minor dissimilar colours. The final result will be a plain, with only one colour.
Rezise grid to same size, completely fill with main colour from source grid.

copy the input grid size. look for the one contiguous colored block that has a few different colored spots on it. make this entire block the one main color. if there are any spots outside of this block make them all black.
Dots in shape filled in with shape colour, dots outside shape turned to black, grid remains same.

Look at the input grid, this will have multiple colours in it, take note of the colour that appears the most. The answer will be the same grid size as the input with the colour shape that has the most coloured squares filled in at the same location and size as the input. If there are other colours on the top of the shape in the input with the most of that colour, these blocks will become the same colour as the shape with the most of that colour. If there is a border around the shape that has the most of the one colour, this border will be put on your grid as one complete coloured border.
Copy the input so that your grid corresponds with the input grid. Inside the predominant solid object, fill any squares that aren't the majority colour with the majority colour.
use grid size from the input then remove any of the different colored squares and then fill in the main square with the same color it originally was
FULL BOX HAVE BEEN FULLY COMPLETED

Resize to match the input grid size.  You're then going to remake the large rectangle in the center.  Make it all the same color and filled in.  Get rid of any of the colored squares that are outside the main object.
i follow the Task demonstration

Change the grid size to the same as the input. There will be individual square objects that don't match the color of the large object. Change the mismatched colors in the large object to the color of the large object so it's a solid object of one color. If there is a black boarder around the object then change the mismatched colors to black to make a solid black boarder.
copy input. keep only the large colored square. delete all other colors. fill in any blank spaces within the shape to make it uniform
Resize the grid to match the sample.  Copy the large shape exactly, size, and color.  Fill with the main color any squares that are a different color
Copy the grid and cells exactly from the input but change any individual differently coloured cells to the same colour of the main object.

copy the input grid size. take the block of solid color and recreate it, filling in any individual spots on the block that were a different color with the main color. make all the other blocks around it black. so you have one solid color in the middle with black blocks around it.
all grid are same colour
Adjust canvas size to match the input. Remove any disparate "dots" or "pixels" that are in front of the background. Final image should only be the background shape or color from the input.
Keept the grid the same size but color all the blocks whatever color is the majority color in that row.

Copy the input image to the answer grid (same size, shapes, and colors). The input features one large, single colored square and some number of differently colored single squares. For single squares that are within the large square, replace them with the same color as the large square to form a single contiguous shape of the same color. Otherwise, simply remove them.
Copy the grid. Remove any single coloured squares and change them to the colour of the background or border that they are positioned on.

if colours aren't touching, then they turn into the colour of what surrounds them. After working this out, I just needed to change the individually coloured squares to the colour that surrounded them.

First resize the grid to be the same as the original. You will see rectangles that are mostly solid colors with dots of different colors in them. Recreate the rectangles in the same size and location as in the original, but make them solid colors using the majority color of the original. Make sure the background is black.
Copy the input grid. Then remove any wrong colored pixels from inside colored objects and the entire grid, or complete the objects if they are missing pixels for their rectangular shapes.
Copy grid, fix the large shapes, fill the black holes or remove the alternate colour blocks from the grid.
Copy the grid size from the original and copy the images over. Fill in the shapes that have any missing blocks using the same colour. For any shaded areas outside the shapes, remove them.
copy from input, change any odd squares to the correct colour of the shapes and keep all background black, submit.

In the input you will see 3 main shapes, each primarily of a single colour. Within this 4 sided shapes and potentially in other cells, there will be a random smattering of cells of a different colour. The solution is to copy the input picture, then amend the colour of any of these cells, to match the main colour of each shape. The solution needs to be 3x 4 sided shapes, so any cells shaded outside of these shapes should be made to be black.

Keep grid size the same. Identify objects that are either square or retangular but with one or two squares coloured differently to the rest of the object and colour them the same as the object. Any squares outside of the objects not coloured black should be coloured black.

Make the grid the same height and width as the original. Copy the large shapes using the same colors into the same locations on the grid. Any single blocks that are black or a different color should be disregarded and those spaces filled in with the color of that shape.

Okay so there should be an obvious back ground color and then there could be very large block grids (or not) but if you see any really small grid dots of like 1 or two squares you should change them to the color of the square those odd shapes are resting on or the background color if the squares are not on a particular large shape.  Again you should use the same grid. Basically when finished it should have the same background color and then all the large shapes in tack without any weird colored dots over the picture.

Copy the grid and objects over exactly.  Look at your objects and fill any holes with the same color as the object.  Single random colored squares should be blacked out.  So in the end your will have whole squares or rectangles in the same colors as the original objects.

The grid remains the same. Copy the test input over. The red squares need to be eliminated and turned the same green as the others. Select each red square and change its color until it matches the others and the entire pattern is green.
The rule here appears to be to remove the dots. So copy the grid over then just fill in the coloring of the dots to match the background color.
Any odd dots inside of squares/rectangles filled in with colour of square/rectangle. Any odd dots in black grid turned black.

The answer grid is same size grid as the input. The only changes from input are the squares that don't match the surrounding objects or background color. These need to change to either match the background or the colored region they are on in the answer grid. It must match the color.
If there are some different dots over the objects or anywhere on the grid, either fill them in or remove them in order to make the objects complete and the grid clear.

Copy the input grid size and objects. Note the spattering of other colored squares over what looks like solid objects. Recolor those to match the squares around it to make what looks like solid squares or rectangular objects keep the original colors.
Copy over the exact input image.  Now change the colour of any individually coloured cells to the colour which matches the object it is on.
Look for any clear shapes, i.e., squares or rectangles. Recreate these and make sure the rest of the image is completely clean, i.e., remove any pixels that appear 'scattered'

Use the same grid size as the input. You should see various large rectangles in the background. In the foreground (overlaying the rectangles), you should see dots of a different colour (you can differentiate as these are only made up of one pixel/part, whereas the other rectangles are many pixels/parts combined). You copy the large rectangles exactly as presented in the input and ignore / remove the coloured dots overlaying the rectangles. Make sure to fill in the large rectangles completely - removing a pixel does not mean the rectangle has a hole, this hole should be filled like the rest of the rectangle.

Use the same grid size as in the input. Identify the shapes within the grid. Colour in any artefacts within the shape to make it solid complete object. Identify any artefacts outside of those shapes that are different to the background and colour those in to match the background.
and i follow the Task demonstration

Use the same grid size as the input. In the input, you will see a design that looks imperfect because it has some "noise"; in other words, it has some randomly colored squares thrown in messing up a neat design of solid shapes. Delete all the unappealing squares (make them black) so that the design is pleasing to the eye with solid colors.

Copy the original image without any of the single color squares moving over, should the be over a color that would complete a shape in the original the single square color changes to the color that would complete the shape
Use the same grid size. Replicate the big shape images in size, colour and position. Remove the singular coloured 1x1 squares.

There will be rectangular outlines of a certain colour, with dots of a different colour randomely around the grid. Recreate in the same grid size the rectangular outlines with the same colours as before for both the outlines and the background. Do not recreate the random dots of coloured noise, instead making them either background or outline as appropriate.
Resize grid to same size. Only copy the shape and colour of shapes that have an inside.
Copy the original grid. Notice how it makes squares and rectangles with a bunch of dots. your taks is to make the dots disapear by making them the same color as the background

Copy the grid size exactly, drawing in the outline of the squares using the same colour, the inside of the shapes is to remain fully the same colour as the background, and there should be no other coloured tiles other than the outline of the shapes
Recreate the grid. Remove any of the single blocks that don't form a square by coloring them black.
Fill the color in square

Keep the grid size and position of the objects the same. Remove any squares that take up less than 4x4 of grid space (usually a single grid square). Color in squares on the grid to complete the perimeter of any broken objects (what look like should be squares or rectangles) with their associated color if any were removed in the small square elimination process.

Use the same grid size as in the input. among the shapes there are big shapes and smaller shapes designed to distract from the big shapes. Remove all smaller shapes and leave only the big shapes in your redraw on your grid.
Copy the grid. Remove anything that is not a complete shape. The background colour should remain the same when removing the "noise".

Press the copy from input button. Then change the colour of any squares which do not make up a perimeter of a shape or match the predominant background colour to the colour of the background. If a perimeter is made up of multiple colours, find what the most predominant colour is and change the shape perimeter to match that colour fully
copy input, turn the blue squares red (except the one that's breaking the green shape, that should be green)

Consider the highest frequency colour in the image, copy those pixels in those positions over to the same size grid in your Output grid.

There are lines forming empty shapes that may or may not be broken by one pixel of a different colour to the shape, copy these shapes over in the same colour and size and position as the Test Input. Replace the single breaks in colour with the shapes main colour.

Both inside and outside of the empty shapes there may be pixels of a low frequency colour, replace these with the highest frequency "background" colour.

There will be one or more 4-sided rings in the grid. These will have the same colour but may appear to be broken by a random coloured grid square. Remove all random coloured squares that do not appear to be part of a "ring" shape. 
Alternative explanation: Replace all randomly placed grid squares and set them to either black or the correct colour to complete a 'ring'.
Use the same size grid as original, recreate the squares and rectangles in their original shape, size and colour. Turn all of the individually-coloured squares to the background colour.
Copy the input grid, including resizing. Delete all individual squares that are not part of a defined linear pattern.
Copy from input the entire image. Change all the free floating squares to black.
Make the grid the same size as the original. Copy the zig-zag pattern from the original, with the same colors in the same squares.

Copy the original grid with all its elements as is. Identify the one connected, coherent line structure found in the grid. First, fill in with black all the cells outside of this coherent structure. Second, identify the primary color of this line. If any cells within the line are not that primary color, change them so that the line is all one color.

Remove any isolated pixels that are not part of the connected zig zag pattern. For isolated pixels in a different color overlapping the zig zag pattern, color them the same color as the pattern. The end result should be an even zig zag pattern of only one color.
use the exact same grid and put the shapes into the same spo, there should be a pattern with random spots, these random spots you want to erase and not have them.
You have a zig-zag shape on the board with random squares filled in the grid in/around the zig-zag. The rule is to remove these spots so only the zig-zag remains

copy from input
color the purple tiles black
Use the same grid size as the input. There will be more than one object in the input as outliers, in your answer, remove the outliers ( keep its colour the same).
Use the same grid size as the task grid and remove all other single blocks around the M-shaped block

make the grid the same size as the example grid, then fill in the floating dots with black. if there are any floating squares over the diagonal stripes fill them in with the same color as the stripes.
Resize the grid to the same size as the main shape. Keep the same pattern but make sure all cells are blue. There should be no grey cells in the figure. Remove the extra cells.

copy the same size grid as the input. copy the same object as the input however irradicate all the grey grid square s and turn them black to make them part of the background.  The two grey squares which are on the blue object need to be turned blue so that it is one continuous blue shape gong down the screen

So the rule here is that on the 2nd image any 'noisy bricks' or bricks that don't match the main colour are removed. In this case, this involved removing the grey bricks

To do this in this example, I used the edit tool and set it to the black colour. I then removed any 'noisy bricks' or grey bricks from outside of the shape. I then changed the colour to blue and replaced any grey bricks from within the shape with blue bricks.
Use the same size grid as original, copy the saw or tooth shape from the original and turn all of it's blocks to the same, original colour. Leave out any of the single squares of colour

use the copy from input button.
Select the same colour in edit mode as the zig zag lines, and replace any colour that is not the colour of the zig zag line with in the zig zag line. So the lines are unbroken and the same colour. 

Replace on the other colours which are not the background colour or the zig zag line colour with the back ground colour. when there is only the one colour zig zag line and the background colour it is complete

Resize the grid to the same size as input, copy the zig zag's size and colour but do not include the dotted shapes scattered around, and fill in the zag if there's any of the other colour blocks covering it.

Adjust the width and height of the grid until it's the same as the input. There should be coloured lines/patterns in the input design, with several dots of a different colour. Copy the design of the input, and "remove" (or change the colour of the dots that are not on the "coloured line" to black) the dots that do not match the input's coloured lines. Make sure that any dots of a differing colour on the coloured lines are the same as the colour of these coloured lines.

There is a zigzag pattern and some other shapes that do not fit the pattern which may or may not be the same color. Remove all of the other colors to make only the zigzag still there at the end, recoloring any shapes that may or may not cover the zigzag as well.

Copy the entire input grid. You should have a continuous shape that consists of triangle shapes lines, on a black background. Then you should have some other boxes filled in with either the same or another color. Keep only the triangle shaped line, and fill in all other boxes with black. If any of the boxes within the triangle shaped line are a different color than the rest, make them all the same. So you have the triangle shaped lines going across your grid, all of the same color, and nothing else but a black background.

Keep the grid the same size as the original. You will see a line going back and forth diagonally, up and down, and you will see a bunch of squares that appear out of place and are not part of the diagonal line. Replicate the line (same size and same color) and delete all the squares that seem out of place. So you are left with just the line running back and forth.
Eliminate the extra dots outside of the zigzag.
Copy the grid exactly as it is presented except for any dots not connected to the line. Make those space blanks.
First, set the grid size the same as the test.  There should be a series of diagonal lines of the same color.  Copy only the diagonal lines in the same color.  Remove any extra squares.
Blocks that are not part of the main pattern are removed.
the blue color of change in task of help in own risk.

Two matching mirrored objects are depicted on either side of the grid, along with several small randomly placed grid squares that can be interpreted as noise, like static on the image of a television screen. Recreate the grid without the noise.
Duplicate the grid and then eliminate all of the orange blocks.

Copy the grid square and the two main shapes. Leave black any single coloured squares and any square attached to the two main shapes that are a different colour. Chaange the colour of any single colours squares that sit on the shapes to match the colour of the shape.
Copy the input onto your submission grid and cover any cells which are just one cell of the same colour not connected to any other cells of the same colour in black
i copied the selection automatically then filled the odd parts black

Make grid sizes exactly same as he input. Copy the input. You will see some patterns which are mirror images. Any grid cell that are not part of those shapes, make it so that it is 1) pattern's or 2)background's color. End up with mirror images in plain color (and same color).

Resize the grid to the same size as the test grid. Find the squares the are connected and make a shape. Fill in the shape with the same color so that it is uniform. Use the same color as the color with the majority of squares. Do not fill in any squares that are not part of the shapes.
Resize grid to match one on left. Identify the two identical objects within grid. Use select to create identical copy within grid. Only use exact match of color of object. Disregard any other colors.
Remove the random single blocks outside the main shapes and change the single blocks inside the main shapes to the same colour as those shapes
Use same grid size, remove the randomly coloured cells and fill in any gaps left within the shape
There will be the two main shapes pointing at each other. Remove all shapes that are other colours, whether outside or inside the shape, until it is just the two big shapes

Use same grid dimensions as input. You are decluttering the input of grid squares that do not belong to a larger object. Your solution only includes two larger objects from the input, keeping their color and dimensions, but no other aspect of the input grid. Each object should be a triangle in shape of one color, with not other cluttering squares around.

The original image is two arrows and some random squares of a different colour. Remove all the random squares. If doing this creates gaps in the two arrows then fill them in with the colour of the arrow, so that the solution is two complete arrows in solid colour and nothing else.

Copy the grid size. Look at the shapes. There should be some larger shapes (shaped like pixelated triangles) with shapes of 1 block in size (of either the same or different colour) around this in an irregular pattern. Delete this and just copy the larger shapes in the same colour as shown.

Use the same 7x15 grid. Copy the same thing except remove all the pink blocks. If the blocks are in the middle of one of the three shapes, make sure you change the color to the color of the shape. All three shapes should be the same.

So the rule here is that the pink bricks must be removed on the 2nd image. I previously called these 'noisy bricks'. 

To do this, I resized the grid to 7X15 to replicate the original and then copied the original image. I then selected the black colour and the edit function. I then filled in all the pink bricks outside of the shapes into black. After this, I selected the relevant shape colour and replaced the pink bricks with the correct colour.

Copy the grid from the original drawing. Remove any spots or dots from the main shapes. Make these larger shapes all one colour. Remove any of the spots that are not from the main shape and make them black too.

Fill any objects so that they are one solid colour, with the colour that covers the majority of the shape being the solid colour. Fill any squares outside of these shapes with black squares so that the objects sit in a black back ground

The rule is that anything apart from the pyramid shapes should be removed. You should copy the input grip using the copy input button. Then use the edit function to remove any square that are not the pyramids - so that is anything around then and also any colour that is not the colour of the pyramnid you are working on - colurs that are overlaying the pyramid shapes, These quares should be changed to the main colour fo the pyramid in question. Use the edit function for this - select edit, select the colour you want to change the square to, then click on the square.

Choose 'Copy from input' button. The grid should consist of shapes of the same size and color. Choose 'Select' and draw a box that contains the entire light blue box. Click the black color to erase it. Choose 'Edit', click the color of the remaining shapes, and click the cells that will make all of the partial shapes into the same shape as the complete shapes.
you need to delete the blue blocks and then make the shapes all the same as the others

Identify the shape in the top left hand corner of the grid. Copy over the grid size and all of the shapes. Remove the light blue box from the grid replacing it with either black squares or the colour of the shape in order to complete the shapes to replicate the one in the top left hand corner.

Set your grid to the same size as the example. You will see coloured squares forming shapes. Each shape is the same, however some shapes are covered by a blanket of different coloured squares. Fill out the same shapes, to the same colour as the example, but leave out the other coloured shapes, instead fill out the shapes as they would be if not for those other coloured squares.
Remove the blue shape from the image. Keep the background shapes in their original form.

Copy input grid. Replace all light blue squares with black. Notice that each shape in the input grid has a pattern, and that a few are incomplete due to the black blocks. Complete the pattern with the same color as the shapes in the input grid
Take first shape on left and duplicate shapes by filling cells with same color and using black for any excess cells. Make the grid size the same as the demonstration.

Okay you need to copy the original grid over and then proceed to remove the light blue squares. Now you need to keep the objects of the other color and they are in fact all the same size and shape so you'll be able to fill them in as some are probably covered up by the blue square. So if there's a T patter or H pattern or whatever pattern that's all you should have showing when you are done as all the shapes have to be the same size and shape and color.

Change the grid size to the input size. There will several objects of the same color and one object that is blue, large and rectangular. Remove the rectangle and then repair the other colored object to it's full shape. The full shape will be the shape with most blocks. Keep the color of the shapes.

Copy the grid.

You should see several shapes of one color, some of which are covered by another color - probably light blue. Remove the light blue shape in its entirety. 

Once the light blue has been removed, find the shapes which were covered by the light blue and match them to the uncovered shapes.
Make a same size grid. Remove the blue rectangle. Now look to the far left pink object. You will make all of the other pink objects the same shape as that one.
Blue was removed and objects exposed.
Copy from input. Get rid of all the blue, and complete the remaining shapes so that they match the shape on the far left of the grid.
Remove the light blue shaded cells and complete the missing cells of the duplicated shapes in the input. Same grid size and keep the colour the same as the replicated shapes too
Duplicate input, remove blue boxes and complete remaining shapes to look the same as the pink shape seen completed in the input
Take note of the prevailing color and shape. Make the rest of the grids black. Then, fill in any missing parts of the main object with its original color in order to complete it.

Copy the grid size and shapes from input. Then complete any of the missing shapes in their respective colors, and remove any of the light blue cells and turn them to black, so that only the "finished" shapes are showing.
Copy input grid. Remove the blue block overlay and complete the pattern below.
make the grid the same size. copy the pattern from the original. Replace the light blue section with a continuation of the pattern. Colours stay the same.
Copy grid from input; Remove the BLOCKS of color; Fill in where shapes / patterns need to be completed
Remove the random blocks of colour and replace so all shapes are the same style and colour

Make sure the grid is 10x10 before starting.  Then look for a diagonal set of blocks.  For each 2x2 block on that diagonal line, copy that block to the solution in the color that is most prevalent in the 2x2 block that matches the position.

Keep the same grid. Color all objects located outside the diagonal big squares black. Adjust color of diagonal big squares so that all big squares are of different colors with color of each big square determined by the color of the square present the most in each large square
Create a diagonal line of 2x2 boxes, color each box with the majority color it started with.

Resize the box to a 10x10 grid. The input box contains 5 items to be copied, as well as "noise" in the form of individual squares, two adjacent squares, or an individual square that is the "wrong" color on top of one of the 5 items. The items to be copied are 4-square boxes running in a diagonal across the input box. Change any individual square's color which is on top of one of the 5 diagonal boxes to the same color as the other three squares in the box. Remove any "noise" around the 5 boxes. Do not change the position or color of the 5 boxes.

This might look overwhelming but its simple. You need a 10 x 10 grid. Ignore the peripheral blocks, you only need to think about the squares which span diagonally. In your solution, only replicate the squares going diagonally, and each individual square needs to be made up of only one colour (the main colour it had in the original). the rest is black. you should have five diagonal cubes, corners meeting.

Copy from input. Remove any single squares do not form part of the 2x2 squares which are diagonally connected. Ensure these remaining 2x2 squares are block colours, if any single blocks within the 2x2 squares are different colours, colour them the same as the majority within that 2x2 block.
Leave just the four boxed squares diagonally and remove all the single coloured squares. The four boxed squares need to be the same colour as the majority of the square is already

Copy over the highest frequency colours ("background") in the same position as the Test Input

Consider any shapes of 4 pixels or larger. Copy these from the Test Input over to a grid of the same size in your Output Solution. Keep them in the same position. Replace the low frequency colours with the highest frequency colour for that shape to make the shape a single colour.

Outside of these shapes replace any pixels that do not match the "background" colour, with the background colour.

Copy the grid and shapes as they are. Change the color of any squares inside the 2x2 squares to match the other three. Any square outside of the 2x2 squares should be changed to black. You will end up with a diagonal line of squares in different colors extending from one corner to the other.
The rule for this task is to remove all the one square colored items.

Keep the grid size the same. There will be a number of square 4x4 boxes along one of the diagonals of the first image, except some solours will be wrong, and there will be added 'noise' around it. Recreate in the second image just the diagonal of 4x4 squares keeping the colours that they should be. Create a grid exactly the same height and width as the original.  Within the mix of shapes are square of 2 by 2 cells. Ignore all other shapes and cells in the original.

The goal is to create a diagonal line made of groupings of 4 blocks in a square. You should be able to identify a diagonal pattern of groupings, each with a dominant color. Change your grid to 14x14 and clean up the test. Change the groupings so each grouping has 1 dominant color, and remove any square that does not fit within the diagonal pattern

Use the same grid size as the input. From the squares going diagonally across the grid, fill in the one square that doesn't match with the other colors to form squares. Fill in the rest of the grid black.

Make your output the same size as your input. The input will have 2x2 squares extending from one corner to another in a diagonal line. Each of these 2x2 squares will touch another by a corner, and each 2x2 square will be composed of 3 grid blocks of one color and 1 grid block of a different color. Make your output entirely black, then, draw these squares and ONLY these squares, such that each square is in the pattern presented in the input BUT, instead of being 3 blocks of a color and 1 block of another color, make them 4 blocks of the color that had 3 blocks of the same color in the input.
DIAGONAL BOXES ARE SAME COLOUR
